title: Geometric dependence of critical-current variation in Al/AlO${\rm _x}$/Al Josephson junctions: a model-based analysis
authors: K. Kakuyanagi, N. Teran, H. Toida, S. Saito
abstract: Achieving uniform critical current across Josephson junctions is essential for the large-scale integration of superconducting quantum circuits. In this work, we statistically analyzed the variation of the critical current of Al/AlO${\rm _x}$/Al junctions using room-temperature tunnel resistance statistics, and identified the dominant contribution among the modeled sources of the variation based on their dependence on geometry and deposition conditions of junctions. Our model-based analysis reveals that fluctuations in the Al film thickness play the dominant role among the modeled contributing factors. Based on this analysis, we found that, in Dolan-bridge double-angle deposition, adopting a deposition angle of 30-degree for bilayer junctions significantly improves uniformity, yielding a relative standard deviation of 1.2% (0.5%) across a 9.75 mm (1.5 mm) square region.
summary: This work investigates the sources of variation in the critical current of Al/AlO_x/Al Josephson junctions essential for quantum circuit scaling. Through model-based statistical analysis, the authors pinpoint film thickness fluctuations as the main cause of variation. They demonstrate that optimizing the deposition angle to 30 degrees significantly improves junction uniformity, providing crucial guidelines for nanofabrication.
main_problem: The primary challenge addressed is achieving uniform critical current across Josephson junctions, which is critical for scaling superconducting quantum circuits.
main_result: The analysis shows that fluctuations in the Al film thickness are the dominant source of critical current variation, and using a 30-degree deposition angle significantly improves junction uniformity.
method: The study combines statistical analysis of room-temperature tunnel resistance with a model-based approach that decomposes the total variance into contributions from different physical sources.
model_or_system: The system is the Al/AlO_x/Al Josephson junction fabricated using the Dolan-bridge technique. The model relates the critical current variation to the variance of the junction's tunnel resistance, decomposing this variance based on geometric factors and film thickness fluctuations.
key_observables: Critical current (I_C), room-temperature tunnel resistance (R_N), relative standard deviation (r.s.d.) of I_C, and the geometric factor f_2(theta, N_L).
important_parameters: Deposition angle (theta), junction width (W), number of deposited layers (N_L), and the size of the measurement region (e.g., 1.5 mm).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes that the variance can be decomposed into statistically independent components, and that thickness fluctuations of different layers are uncorrelated.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate cross-sectional schematics of the junctions, show normalized RMS variation vs. junction width, and present resistance histograms for different sized measurement regions demonstrating decreasing r.s.d. with smaller regions.
paper_structure: The paper establishes the need for uniform I_C, models the variance of the junction resistance as a sum of independent components (geometry-independent, thickness-related, etc.), derives a geometric factor quantifying thickness effects, and then uses statistical analysis on fabricated junctions to identify thickness variation as dominant and quantify the improvement achieved by specific deposition angles.
why_interesting: While focused on superconducting materials, the rigorous statistical modeling of geometric variation and the quantitative link between deposition parameters (angle) and device uniformity are highly relevant to engineering quantum devices where nanoscale precision is paramount.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Cross-sectional schematic of the Al/AlOx/Al bilayer junc- tion formed by the Dolan-bridge (purple) double-angle deposition. The blue (green) layer represents the ﬁrst (second) deposited Al ﬁlm. The surfaces highlighted in red correspond to the junction interfaces with area S, and the perimeter of these surfaces corresponds to the junction-edge length L. The magniﬁed schematic illustrates the rela- tionship among the deposition angle θ, the ﬁlm-thickness ﬂuctuation δd, and the junction interfaces (red and orange). The sidewall sur- face and the top surface constitute the junction interfaces. When the ﬁlm thickness increases by δd, the junction interface shifts from the line shown...

FIG. 2. Geometry dependence of the measured normalized RMS variation and the corresponding model ﬁtting results. White (black) circles represent the measured data for the bilayer (trilayer) junc- tions, and the error bars are estimated from the unbiased sample vari- ance. The red (blue) solid line represents the model ﬁtting results for the bilayer (trilayer) junctions. The dashed horizontal lines indi- cate the asymptotic values of the normalized RMS variation for the bilayer and trilayer junctions, which correspond to the contribution from ﬁlm-thickness ﬂuctuations. The magenta (cyan) shaded region indicates the uncertainty of the ﬁtted curve for the bilayer (trilayer) junctions,...

FIG. 3. Results of room-temperature resistance measurements for the 9.75 mm square junction array. (a) Two-dimensional map of the measured resistance. The four yellow points correspond to open or shorted junctions, which are excluded from the statistical analysis. (b) Histogram of the resistance values for all valid junctions. The red dashed line is a Gaussian curve shown as a guide to the eye.

FIG. 4. (a) Schematic of three representative regions: 4.5 mm square (red), 3.0 mm square (green), and 1.5 mm square (cyan). The dataset is the same as that in Fig. 3. These regions are chosen to minimize standard deviations. (b–d) Corresponding histograms of the measured resistance values in these three regions. The dashed lines are Gaussian curves shown as guides to the eye.
